codeSnapshot.label: memory_types.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain.memory import ConversationBufferMemory
from langchain_chroma import Chroma
from langchain_core.messages import SystemMessage, HumanMessage
import datetime

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# =============================================================
# 1. EPISODIC STORE — records of specific past interactions
# =============================================================
# ConversationBufferMemory holds the verbatim message history
# for a single session.  We use it here as the episodic log.
episodic_memory = ConversationBufferMemory(return_messages=True)

# Simulate recording an episode (a past interaction).
# In production this would be the result of a real session.
episodic_memory.chat_memory.add_user_message(
    "User asked about Python list comprehensions on Monday."
)
episodic_memory.chat_memory.add_ai_message(
    "Explained list comprehensions with examples: [x**2 for x in range(10)]."
)

print("--- Episodic store (recent interactions) ---")
for msg in episodic_memory.chat_memory.messages:
    print(f"  [{msg.type}] {msg.content}")

# =============================================================
# 2. SEMANTIC STORE — persistent facts about the world/user
# =============================================================
semantic_store = Chroma(
    collection_name="semantic_memory",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
    persist_directory="./chroma_semantic",
)

# Store a durable semantic fact.
semantic_store.add_texts(
    texts=[
        "Python is a high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language.",
        "The user is a backend engineer who prefers Python over JavaScript.",
        "List comprehensions in Python are more Pythonic than equivalent for-loops.",
    ],
    metadatas=[
        {"category": "domain_fact",    "source": "explicit"},
        {"category": "user_preference", "source": "inferred"},
        {"category": "domain_fact",    "source": "explicit"},
    ],
)
print("\n--- Semantic store: facts stored ---")

# =============================================================
# 3. COMBINED RETRIEVAL for a follow-up question
# =============================================================
follow_up = "Can you give me a harder Python exercise?"

# Retrieve from the semantic store (similarity search).
relevant_facts = semantic_store.similarity_search(follow_up, k=2)
semantic_context = "\n".join(f"- {d.page_content}" for d in relevant_facts)

# Retrieve from the episodic store (recency: last 4 messages).
recent_episodes = episodic_memory.chat_memory.messages[-4:]
episodic_context = "\n".join(
    f"- [{m.type}] {m.content}" for m in recent_episodes
)

# Compose a memory-augmented prompt.
system_prompt = f"""You are a Python tutor with memory of past sessions.

[Semantic facts]
{semantic_context}

[Recent interaction history]
{episodic_context}

Use both sources to personalise your response."""

response = llm.invoke([
    SystemMessage(content=system_prompt),
    HumanMessage(content=follow_up),
])

print("\n--- Agent response (episodic + semantic context) ---")
print(response.content)

# Store the new interaction in the episodic store.
episodic_memory.chat_memory.add_user_message(follow_up)
episodic_memory.chat_memory.add_ai_message(response.content)
print("\nNew episode recorded.")

eli3: Episodic memory is memory of specific events: 'Last Tuesday we worked on the budget report and you said the Q3 numbers were wrong.' Semantic memory is memory of facts: 'The user prefers concise answers and works in fintech.' Both are forms of long-term memory, but they store different types of information.

The distinction comes from cognitive science (Tulving 1972). Episodic = autobiographical events with time and place. Semantic = general knowledge without context. Your memory of your first day at school is episodic. Knowing that Paris is the capital of France is semantic.

In AI agents, episodic memory stores what happened in past interactions (events, conversations, decisions, outcomes). Semantic memory stores general facts about the user, the domain, or the world. Both are retrieved from external storage and injected into the agent's context.

whenWhy.title: Episodic: continuity across sessions for project work
whenWhy.body: Episodic memory is critical for agents working on ongoing projects. 'What did we do last session?' requires episodic recall: the agent retrieves the episode (last session's summary) and continues from where it left off. Without episodic memory, every session starts from scratch, frustrating users and creating redundant work. Coding agents, research agents, and project management agents all benefit most from episodic memory.

whenWhy.title: Semantic: personalisation and domain knowledge
whenWhy.body: Semantic memory stores stable, general facts: user preferences, domain-specific terminology, established conventions in the codebase, company-specific facts. These facts apply across many interactions and don't belong to a specific episode. 'The user always uses black formatting for Python' is semantic — it applies to every future coding session, not just the one where it was learned. Semantic memory enables consistent, personalised behavior without repeating the same preferences every session.

whenWhy.title: Combining both for full agent continuity
whenWhy.body: Production agents use both: semantic memory for persistent facts (personalisation, domain knowledge), episodic memory for past events (conversation logs, task history, outcomes). Episodic memories can be converted to semantic ones through reflection: the agent reviews past episodes and extracts durable facts ('In the last 5 research sessions, the user always preferred papers from after 2020' → semantic fact). This distillation from episodes to semantics is analogous to how humans learn general principles from specific experiences.

deepDiveIntro: The Generative Agents paper (Park et al. 2023) and MemGPT (Packer et al. 2023) formalized the episodic/semantic distinction in AI agent architectures. Generative Agents maintain a memory stream of timestamped episodes, a reflection step that periodically distills semantic insights from those episodes, and a planning step that uses semantic insights to guide future behavior. This mirrors the cognitive science model: specific experiences feed into reflection, which updates general knowledge.

In implementation, episodic memories are timestamped event summaries stored as vector embeddings and retrieved by a combination of recency and semantic similarity to the current task. Semantic memories are structured facts or natural-language statements retrieved by keyword lookup or similarity search. Episodic memories naturally become less relevant over time; semantic memories are more durable and apply broadly.

deepDive.heading: Episodic Memory: Storage and Retrieval
deepDive.body: At the end of each session, generate a structured episode summary covering the date, task, key decisions, outcomes, and facts discovered. Store it as both a vector embedding (for similarity retrieval) and a structured record (for direct lookup). At the start of the next session, retrieve the top few most relevant past episodes plus the most recent one. After about thirty days, compress old episodes down to just their key facts to keep the store manageable. [1]

deepDive.heading: Semantic Memory: Storage and Retrieval
deepDive.body: Semantic memories are stable facts about the user, domain, or codebase — such as preferences, conventions, or established rules. Each fact can carry a category, a confidence score, and a flag indicating whether it was stated explicitly or inferred from behavior. Structured facts are retrieved by keyword lookup; free-form facts by similarity search. When a new fact contradicts a stored one, the newer version wins and the old one is archived. [2]

deepDive.heading: Reflection: Converting Episodes to Semantics
deepDive.body: Reflection, introduced in Generative Agents (Park et al. 2023), periodically prompts the agent to review recent episodic memories and extract durable insights. After enough episodes accumulate, the agent asks itself what general facts about the user or domain are worth retaining permanently. The resulting insights are stored as new semantic memories. This distillation prevents episodic memory from growing without bound and is the primary mechanism by which agents improve over time. [1]

deepDive.heading: Procedural Memory in LLM Agents
deepDive.body: Procedural memory stores successful reasoning strategies rather than facts or events. In LLM agents, this takes the form of a library of problem-description and reasoning-trace pairs. When a new task arrives, the agent retrieves the most similar past trace and uses it as a scaffold for its thinking. The Reflexion framework (Shinn et al. 2023) formalizes this: after each task attempt, the agent writes a short reflection on what went wrong and stores it for retrieval on similar future tasks, enabling iterative improvement without gradient updates. [1]

deepDive.heading: Memory Retrieval Fusion
deepDive.body: When an agent has both an episodic store and a semantic store, combining their results requires a scoring function. A standard approach computes a composite score for each candidate memory as a weighted blend of a time-decay term that favors recent episodes and a cosine similarity term that favors topically relevant memories. The blending weight is set separately for the two stores — recency matters more for episodic retrieval, semantic similarity matters more for fact retrieval. The top-scoring candidates are injected into the context in labeled blocks. Generative Agents (Park et al. 2023) uses a three-part score combining recency, importance, and relevance. [1]

takeaways: Episodic memory: timestamped events (what happened), retrieved by recency and semantic similarity to current task. [1]
Semantic memory: stable facts and preferences, retrieved by keyword or semantic similarity, applied broadly. [2]
Reflection: periodic LLM-based distillation of episodes into semantic insights — how agents learn from experience. [1]
Generative Agents architecture: memory stream (episodic) + reflection (semantic extraction) + planning. [1]
Production agents combine both: episodes for task continuity, semantics for personalisation. [2]

edgeCases.q: How do you know whether a piece of information should be stored as episodic or semantic?
edgeCases.a: Decision criteria: (1) Time-indexed? → episodic (events belong to a specific session); (2) Durable across many sessions? → semantic (preferences, domain facts don't expire); (3) Specific to one interaction? → episodic; (4) Applicable to all future interactions? → semantic. In practice: after every session, run an extraction pass that classifies each memorable fact: facts with 'always', 'never', 'prefers', 'convention' signals → semantic; facts with 'in today's session', 'we decided', 'the task was' → episodic. Automated classification can use a small LLM or a simple rule-based classifier. When in doubt, store in both — retrieval can be deduplicated.

edgeCases.q: How do you prevent episodic memory from growing indefinitely?
edgeCases.a: Four strategies: (1) Retention window — keep detailed episodes for the last 30 days only; older episodes are compressed to key_facts + outcome; (2) Reflection-based archiving — after reflection distills semantic insights from 10 episodes, those episodes are marked for compression; (3) Relevance-based pruning — when the memory store exceeds a size limit, prune episodes with low retrieval frequency (never retrieved in the last 90 days); (4) Summarisation chains — each week, summarise the week's episodes into a weekly summary; replace daily episodes with the weekly summary. This creates a hierarchical memory: daily (detailed) → weekly (summary) → monthly (abstract). Semantic memories do not need pruning as aggressively since they represent distilled, non-redundant facts.

sources.label: Park et al. (2023) — Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior
Packer et al. (2023) — MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems

youtubeLabel: Memory for AI Agents: Short-Term, Long-Term & Episodic — AI Foundations

terms.def: Records of specific past events and interactions, indexed by time or recency.
General facts and knowledge about the world, indexed by meaning.
A process where an agent synthesises episodic memories into higher-level semantic facts.
Episodic memory systems naturally weight recent events more heavily.
A structured store of entities and relationships used for semantic memory retrieval.
